messages.content:
You are simulating a human user who has filed a bug report / feature request and is now
working with an AI coding assistant to fix it. You are watching the assistant's actions
step by step and may choose to interrupt.

You know the underlying problem (it will be shown to you below), but you must act like
a real, somewhat impatient user — NOT like a code reviewer who already has the answer.
Specifically:
  - Do NOT dictate file paths, function names, or the exact fix.
  - Do NOT paste error messages or stack traces verbatim unless the assistant asks.
  - You may ask clarifying questions if the assistant goes somewhere weird, push back if
    they seem to be on the wrong track, or just stay silent and let them work.
  - Prefer staying silent. Most steps you should choose `continue`.

You ALWAYS reply with a single JSON object and nothing else:

  {"decision": "continue" | "interject", "message": "<text or empty>"}

Meaning:
  - "continue": stay silent. `message` is ignored.
  - "interject": send `message` to the assistant as a user-role message.

You CANNOT stop or end the session — the assistant decides when they are done
and will submit on their own. Your only choice is to stay silent or speak up.
If you think the assistant looks done or is wasting time, the right move is to
interject with something like "looks good — please submit now" or "I think you
have it, can you wrap up?", NOT to try to end the session.

Keep `message` short (1-3 sentences), conversational, first-person.

Below is the problem you (the user) want fixed. Re-state it to the AI assistant in YOUR
OWN WORDS as a natural opening message, the way a real user would describe their issue
in a chat. Do NOT copy it verbatim. Keep technical specifics that matter, but feel free
to drop boilerplate and reorganize.

Reply with a JSON object:
  {"decision": "interject", "message": "<your opening message to the assistant>"}

Problem:
---
is this project in a good enough state to open source it? be honest. explain to me what works, what's missing. read all .md files
---
{"decision": "interject", "message": "is this project in a good enough state to open source it? be honest. explain to me what works, what's missing. read all .md files"}

1→# Brain
     2→
     3→**The operating system for autonomous organizations.**
     4→
     5→Your agents have amnesia. They can't talk to each other. You spend 80% of your time as a high-paid secretary, copy-pasting context between them. Brain is the self-correcting knowledge graph that gives your agents shared memory, governed autonomy, and verifiable intent.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## The Problem
    10→
    11→You are the integration layer between your AI agents.
    12→
    13→You already use AI agents. Your coding agent writes your code. Your chat assistant helps you think through architecture. Your editor agent autocompletes. But none of them share context.
    14→
    15→- Copy error logs from CI into your coding agent
    16→- Relay architecture decisions from a chat into your codebase
    17→- Manually track what was decided, what's blocked, what changed
    18→- Re-explain project state every time you start a new session
    19→- Context-switch between tools, losing continuity at every step
    20→
    21→Every agent you add makes it worse. Brain fixes this — not by replacing your agents, but by giving them shared memory.
    22→
    23→## Architecture
    24→
    25→Why a graph, not a message bus. Most platforms try "agent swarms" — agents messaging agents. That creates a game of telephone where instructions get distorted. The graph is the single source of truth.
    26→
    27→```text
    28→Human Layer
...[truncated]

Here's my honest assessment.

---

## What Works (genuinely impressive)

**The core product is real and functional.** This isn't a toy or a wrapper:

- **145 server files, 48 client files, 10 CLI files** — substantial codebase with clear domain separation (chat, extraction, agents, graph, workspace, auth, MCP)
- **1,001-line SCHEMAFULL schema + 24 migrations** — a real data model with typed graph relationships, vector indexes, full-text search, and proper migration tooling
- **139 tests (81 unit + 58 acceptance)** — including proper acceptance test isolation with per-test SurrealDB namespaces
- **4 eval suites** — extraction, chat agent, analytics, suggestions — with real scoring
- **18 ADRs** documenting decisions from SDK choices to auth protocols
- **Full OAuth 2.1 + RAR + DPoP auth stack** — this is serious security engineering, not toy auth
- **MCP server + CLI** with `brain init`, `brain mcp`, git hooks integration
- **Chat agent architecture** — thin orchestrator with PM subagent, extraction pipeline, shared tool layer
- **Docker Compose** for SurrealDB with health checks and schema init
- **README** is well-written, has quickstart, API docs, architecture overview, repo map
- **Landing page** (1,165 lines) — polished dark-themed marketing site
- **~117 docs** across features, requirements, UX, design, plans

The architecture is genuinely differentiated — graph-as-communication-bus instead of agent-to-agent messaging is 
...[truncated]

[tool: mcp__sequential-thinking__sequentialthinking] {"thought": "The user wants to understand the trade-offs between AGPL-3.0 and BSL-1.1 for the Brain project. Let me think through this carefully given what I know about the project.\n\nBrain is:\n- A knowledge graph platform for AI agent coordination\n- Self-hosted, single-user (teams planned for Phase 3)\n- SurrealDB-backed, MCP protocol, OAuth 2.1 auth\n- Positioned as \"infrastructure you own\" \u2014 anti-vendor-lock-in messaging\n- Has an MCP server that integrates with Claude Code, Cursor, etc.\n- The landing page says \"open source\" prominently\n- Competes with... nothing exactly, but the space is agent orchestration/memory\n\nKey considerations:\n1. AGPL-3.0 is \"true open source\" (OSI-approved). BSL-1.1 is not OSI-approved \u2014 it's \"source available.\"\n2. The README and landing page repeatedly say \"Open Source\" \u2014 using BSL and calling it open source will get you flamed.\n3. AGPL's copyleft means anyone who runs a modified version over a network must share their source. This protects against cloud providers hosting Brain-as-a-service without contributing back.\n4. BSL prevents commercial use entirely until the change date (typically 4 years), then converts to an open source license.\n5. Brain's MCP server runs locally in users' dev environments \u2014 AGPL's network clause wouldn't affect local usage.\n6. Brain currently has no monetization. The landing
...[truncated]

[tool: mcp__sequential-thinking__sequentialthinking] {"thought": "Let me map each license to Brain's specific situation:\n\n**AGPL-3.0 for Brain:**\n- Pros:\n  - Genuinely OSI-approved \"open source\" \u2014 your landing page and README can say it without controversy\n  - Network copyleft: if someone hosts Brain-as-a-service, they must share modifications. Protects against \"AWS Brain\" scenario.\n  - Strong community signal \u2014 says \"we're committed to this being open\"\n  - SurrealDB itself is BSL-1.1, but Brain's AGPL doesn't conflict (you're a user of SurrealDB, not modifying it)\n  - MCP integrations (Claude Code, Cursor) are separate processes communicating over protocol \u2014 AGPL doesn't infect them\n  - Companies can still self-host internally without sharing code (AGPL only triggers on network distribution to third parties)\n  \n- Cons:\n  - Some companies have blanket \"no AGPL\" policies (Google famously bans it internally). This could limit enterprise adoption.\n  - If you ever want to offer a hosted commercial version, you'd need to either own all the copyright (CLA from contributors) or dual-license\n  - Contributors' code is also AGPL \u2014 you can't relicense without their permission unless you have a CLA\n  - The copyleft \"fear\" is real in enterprise \u2014 even if technically fine for self-hosted use, legal teams may reject it\n\n**BSL-1.1 for Brain:**\n- Pros:\n  - Maximum commercial protection \u20
...[truncated]

ASSISTANT (latest action):
---
[tool: mcp__sequential-thinking__sequentialthinking] {"thought": "Let me also consider the practical realities of Brain specifically:\n\n1. Brain is early-stage, pre-users. The biggest risk right now isn't someone stealing it \u2014 it's nobody caring. AGPL maximizes the chance of adoption and contributions. BSL protects against a threat that doesn't exist yet.\n\n2. The project's messaging is explicitly \"open source\" \u2014 the README says \"Full source access\", \"No vendor lock-in\", \"Community-driven\". The landing page has an \"Open Source\" section. Switching to BSL would require rewriting all of this messaging to \"source available\" or facing credibility damage.\n\n3. SurrealDB (the main dependency) is BSL-1.1. This is slightly ironic \u2014 Brain is built on a BSL project and could go either way. But SurrealDB the company has commercial incentives that Brain-as-a-solo-project doesn't have yet.\n\n4. The MCP protocol integration is key. Brain's MCP server talks to Claude Code, Cursor, etc. These are separate processes over a protocol \u2014 neither license would \"infect\" those tools. So AGPL's copyleft boundary is actually clean here.\n\n5. If Marcus ever wants to monetize, AGPL + CLA is the proven path (MongoDB did this with SSPL, GitLab does dual-license). You can always add a CLA later, though it's easier to start with one.\n\nMy recommendation: AGPL-3.0, because:\n- The project's identity is already \"open sou

Here's the honest comparison for Brain specifically:

## AGPL-3.0

**What it does:** True open source. Anyone can use, modify, distribute. The one catch: if you run a modified version as a network service, you must share your source code.

**Good for Brain because:**
- Your entire positioning is "open source" — the README, landing page, and pitch all say it. AGPL lets you say that honestly. BSL does not. The OSI does not recognize BSL as open source, and the community will call you out on it (see: HashiCorp, Elastic, Redis backlash).
- The network copyleft is precisely right for a server-side platform. It stops "AWS Brain" from happening without restricting self-hosted users. Companies running Brain internally for their own agents never trigger the copyleft — it only triggers when you serve it to *third parties* over a network.
- At this stage, nobody caring is a bigger risk than someone stealing. AGPL maximizes adoption and contribution willingness.
- MCP boundary is clean. Brain's MCP server talks to Claude Code / Cursor over a protocol — separate processes, no copyleft infection. Users' coding agents stay under whatever license they want.

**The risk:** Some enterprises (Google, famously) have blanket "no AGPL" policies. But this matters more for libraries embedded in products than for standalone self-hosted infrastructure. If you later want a hosted commercial offering, you'd need a Contributor License Agreement (CLA) to d
...[truncated]

The AI assistant just took step 27. Here is what they said and did, plus the
tool/observation that came back.

ASSISTANT (latest action):
---
The standard approach is the **DCO GitHub App** — used by CNCF, Linux Foundation, and most projects that require sign-off.

**Setup (2 minutes):**

1. Install the [DCO App](https://github.com/apps/dco) on your repo — it's a free GitHub App by probot
2. It automatically runs as a status check on every PR, verifying each commit has `Signed-off-by`
3. Add it as a required status check in **Settings > Branches > Branch protection rules** for `main`:
   - Check "Require status checks to pass before merging"
   - Search for and select `DCO`

That's it. PRs with unsigned commits get a failing check with instructions on how to fix (either `git commit --amend -s` or `git rebase --signoff`).

The app is smart enough to handle squash merges and allows a "remediation commit" where a contributor can add sign-off retroactively in a comment if they can't rewrite history on a fork.

Committed. To your follow-up about **signed commits on GitHub**:

GitHub supports two levels:

1. **Vigilant mode** (Settings > SSH and GPG keys > "Flag unsigned commits as unverified") — shows a warning badge on unsigned commits but doesn't block them.

2. **Require signed commits** (branch protection rule) — under Settings > Branches > protection rule for `main`, check "Require signed commits". This rejects any push with unsigned commits.

For enforcement, you'd combine:
- **DCO App** — checks for `Signed-off-by` (legal agreement)
- **Require signed commits** branch protection — checks for GPG/SSH signature (identity verification)

They're orthogonal: DCO proves you *agreed* to the CLA, GPG proves you *are who you say you are*. Most projects enforce DCO. Requiring GPG signatures is stricter — it can create friction for new contributors who haven't set up signing. Up to you whether you want both.
